findings.message: ADR D4.3 says the CI path can get fail-closed path safety by calling `reject_symlinks_below` before `read_to_string`, but that helper only detects filesystem symlinks. A checked-out git submodule appears as a normal directory, so `track/items/<id>/spec.json` inside a submodule would still be read successfully even though the truth table later says case #18 is BLOCKED. The plan needs an explicit CI-side submodule guard or the fail-closed table is overstated.

The approved acceptance criterion for `validate_branch_ref` no longer requires rejecting control characters, even though D4.2 and task T004 describe control-char rejection as part of the contract. An implementation can satisfy the current spec while still accepting refs with embedded `\n`/NUL, leaving the `origin/{branch}:...` pre-validation incomplete.

`spec.json` still cites `.claude/rules/07-dev-environment.md` and `track/tech-stack.md` as direct sources for the layer/build requirement instead of tracing that requirement back through this ADR. That breaks the stated ADR-only source attribution for this track and means an external convention edit can change the approved basis of the spec without any ADR update.

The ADR is self-contradictory about layer ownership: the Context says this redesign will place the gate logic under `libs/infrastructure/src/verify/`, but D5/D9 and the rest of the track move orchestration into `libs/usecase`. Leaving the source-of-truth ADR split like this makes it easy to implement the gate in infrastructure again and violate the intended hexagonal boundary.

D2.0 defines `validate_branch_ref` as a domain API returning `RefValidationError`, but D4.2 redefines the same function as `Result<(), GateError>`. That ambiguity leaks a gate-specific error type back into the domain contract and gives implementers two incompatible signatures to follow.

The T004 acceptance criterion no longer covers control-character rejection, even though D4.2 and task T004 both require `validate_branch_ref` to reject control bytes. As written, the spec can be satisfied without testing one of the fail-closed branch-name cases the ADR calls out.

The out-of-scope race-condition item hardcodes `SEC-18` as the follow-up TODO id. The briefing explicitly requires describing this race without assigning a SEC number because numbering is deferred; if another SEC is created first, this plan will immediately point at the wrong follow-up identifier.

T004 now requires `validate_branch_ref` to reject both `:` and control characters, but the planned domain test budget is still only `D14–D20`, and the ADR matrix behind those IDs never exercises either input. As written, the implementation can satisfy the tracked tests while leaving two fail-closed branches unverified.

The spec was updated to defer the wait-and-merge follow-up to 'the next available SEC number', but D7 in the ADR still hardcodes `SEC-18` as the next slot. That leaves the planning artifacts inconsistent and reintroduces the stale-numbering problem this cleanup was supposed to remove.

`verification.md` still defines T004 as only `D14–D20 tests`, even though the ADR, spec, and metadata were expanded to require `D21` (`:`) and `D22` (control character) rejection. As written, this track can be signed off without verifying the two newly added fail-closed branch-ref cases.
